Now, one of the hardest things in marketing, in paid marketing, in organic marketing, is knowing exactly where your time and money is best spent. And this is amplified with Facebook ads because the more you scale, the more chance that customers have multiple touch points. So what do I mean by that? Here's an example. If you see something in your newsfeed and you click on it, whether it's an ad or an organic post, if there is a sale at the end of it, what's the chance is that you click on other ways to get into that site before you purchase? So if you see a newsfeed ad, you click the ad, you come back later, you see an Instagram ad, you click that ad and you purchase, that's when you need to know about attribution because your campaigns will show that Instagram drove the sale. But where is the data that says, actually, the first click came from Facebook, the last click from Instagram, therefore, the strategy should be that you should run both, right? If you just look at that last click data, then you might make the wrong decision. Drop your budgets on Facebook, maybe even stop advertising on Facebook because clearly the data is telling you that Instagram's working better. If you want more conversions, return on ad spend is better, etc. But that would be disastrous. Now, this is also where some of the scaling strategies end up falling over. So I really want you to understand attribution before you even get to scaling, let alone the optimization stage, because if, for example, you see a particular segment, so let's say Instagram or Facebook or mobile or desktop, which is working better, you have to consider whether that platform alone is the reason why you're driving sales. I've seen ad accounts where people will do that analysis and they'll see, for example, desktop is converting better than mobile and therefore we need to spend more. It's short-sighted because, for example, desktop doesn't have as many users as mobile. So if you're seeing higher conversions on desktop, maybe it's your retargeting ads that are causing those sales to come in, but the top of funnel is coming through on mobile. So there are so many things to consider when it comes to attribution. With all of this in mind, how do you track everything that's going on? Well, there are some ways. Now, first of all, Facebook launched their own attribution tool, I think back in 2018. So if you go into Business Manager, you can select attribution from the top. The process of setting attribution up isn't easy. You have to go through a process. You have to set up a line of business. You have to associate your pixels and ad account and everything together so that Facebook can start collecting that data and there'll be a link below to go through and how to set that up and the technicalities of that as well, which is best served by Facebook. Now once you've got that set up, you get access to data across your pixel. This is really important to understand because as I've explained many times in this training program, the pixel data is collecting everything that's coming into your website. So it's not just the ads that you're driving that the pixel is learning from. It's your organic traffic. It's your Google search. It's your affiliates. Everything that comes in, essentially Facebook can track it if you've got the pixel installed. So in the same way, if you've got Google Analytics, Google Analytics can see all your data, Facebook has very similar data as well. So this first image at the top just shows the split of traffic between paid, organic and direct, which Facebook has identified. This second screenshot on the left is also taken from Facebook shows a breakdown by campaign. This is a really cool way of seeing where your cells are attributed from based on particular models. So you can see, for example, what the last click and the view of what Facebook has in its own data setup on what these conversions look like in the touchpoints. Then you've got this image on the right, which is actually from Google Analytics and it's part of their suite of attribution tools. When I'm analyzing my data from Facebook, I will go deep. If I really want to get into it, I'd go deep into Google Analytics because I find that the models work there really well. And Facebook are making enhancements to their own attribution tools to make it a lot more user-friendly. And I imagine as time goes by, it will become a lot more integrated with that. It's managed right now. It's a completely separate tool to enable you to get better insights on your decision-making. That said, on a day-to-day basis, I track my attribution at a much higher level because you don't need to be in the data of exactly what's being clicked where on a day-to-day basis, but you need to look at the trends. And this is a big thing about the whole performance side of the BPM method is spotting trends to be able to be enabling you to spend your time far more effectively finding problems, identifying solutions, and doing something about them. So here's the thing. If you're spending your time searching for the insights in all of the data and you could spend days and days in data and not find any insights, that's not a great use of your time. However, if you have the dashboards and the insights and you know what you're looking for, then you can make faster decisions. And in the subsequent videos, I'll show you what we set up on a day-to-day basis and what we look at as well.
